definition: A three-phase intraday price delivery model. Accumulation: price consolidates in a range, building liquidity. Manipulation: price sweeps liquidity in the opposite direction of the true move, triggering stops. Distribution: price delivers strongly in the true direction, completing the daily candle structure. The manipulation leg height projects distribution targets using standard deviation levels.
identification_rules: Accumulation is identified as a consolidation range, typically forming during the Asian session or early in a session before a directional move.

Manipulation is identified when price makes an opposing move against the anticipated direction — e.g., a push lower before a bullish move, or a push higher before a bearish move — often occurring at key session times such as 8:30 or 9:30 EST.
Manipulation is confirmed when price closes back over (bullish) or below (bearish) a series of same-direction candles, forming an order block at the manipulation extreme.

Distribution is identified as the strong directional expansion following the manipulation sweep, typically delivered during the New York AM session (08:30–12:00 EST), targeting prior liquidity levels or HTF PD arrays.

The full AMD sequence maps to the Open-Low-High-Close (bullish) or Open-High-Low-Close (bearish) structure of the daily candle, with London forming the manipulation wick and New York delivering the distribution leg.
If accumulation range is unclear, first identify a sharp manipulation move (aggressive breakout and quick return), then look left to find the consolidation range.
Manipulation leg must be followed by an aggressive return into the range; if price fails to return, it is not manipulation.
Distribution phase can be confirmed by a Change In State Of Delivery (CISD) — price closes beyond a series of same-direction candles' opens.
Project distribution targets by applying standard deviation levels (e.g., -2 to -2.5 or +2 to +2.5) to the manipulation leg height.
The model is fractal and applies across timeframes: daily, 4H, 15M, 5M, 1M.
Session mapping: Asian session (20:00–00:00 NY) = accumulation; London session (02:00–05:00 NY) = manipulation; New York AM (07:00–10:00 forex, 08:30–11:00 indices) = distribution.
trading_implication: Enter after manipulation confirmation (close beyond same-direction candles forming order block) in the distribution direction. Stop beyond manipulation extreme. Target minimum 2R or standard deviation projections (-2 to -2.5, -4) aligned with PD arrays (FVG, OB, liquidity). Alternatively, enter on reversal setups during manipulation (Turtle Soup, Inversion FVG) or on continuation during distribution. Require higher timeframe bias and multi-timeframe alignment.
common_mistakes: Entering during manipulation in the direction of the sweep rather than waiting for confirmation of distribution direction.
Misidentifying a retracement during New York as new manipulation rather than part of distribution.
Failing to wait for a close beyond same-direction candles before entering.
Entering on manipulation leg retracement without waiting for Order Block or CISD confirmation.
Assuming manipulation when price fails to aggressively return to the range after breakout.
Over-focusing on finding accumulation range instead of first identifying sharp manipulation behavior.
Trading AMD without higher timeframe narrative (Draw on Liquidity / Bias).
Mistaking range-bound price action for distribution before manipulation sweep occurs.
Misinterpreting London breakout as true direction, entering prematurely before reversal.
Forcing trades during London close (10:00–12:00) when price may be range-bound.
Trying to track all reversal confirmations (Turtle Soup, Inversion FVG, CISD, Breaker Block, FVG) simultaneously, causing analysis overload.
Entering without low timeframe alignment with higher timeframe direction.
merge_notes: All three projects are ICT school; no school conflict. The Education project definition was in Chinese; translated and synthesized. The Foundation project provided more detailed identification rules and common mistakes, which were merged. All rules and mistakes are preserved from inputs.
